evals.prompt: We have 8,000 monthly sessions on our PDP at 2% conversion. We want to test a new headline. Is it worth running?
evals.expected_output: Likely not at common effect sizes. Walks the MDE-sample-size relationship: 2% baseline, 10% relative lift needs ~750k visitors per variant. At 8k monthly sessions, the test cannot reach significance in a year. Recommends focusing on high-leverage changes that don't need statistical proof (value-prop reframe per cro) or running as a directional pilot without significance claim. References cro for the diagnostic approach.
assertions: Walks the MDE-sample-size relationship
Computes required sample size for 10% lift at 2% baseline
Recommends not running as a significance-claiming test
Offers directional pilot or high-leverage non-tested change as alternative
References cro for the diagnostic approach
Output names a single highest-leverage next move

evals.prompt: We've been peeking at our A/B test daily and saw significance at day 4. Should we ship?
evals.expected_output: Probably not. Walks the sequential-testing-without-peeking framework. Notes that daily peeking with stop-on-significance inflates false-positive rate to 25-35% under classical inference. Asks whether the testing tool uses sequential methodology. If not, recommends letting the test run to planned sample size before deciding. Explains why peeking inflates false positives.
assertions: Pushes back on shipping based on day-4 peek
Walks the sequential-testing-without-peeking framework
Notes daily peeking inflates false-positive rate to 25-35%
Asks whether the tool uses sequential methodology
Recommends running to planned sample size if classical inference

evals.prompt: Help me write a strong hypothesis for testing a new homepage hero.
evals.expected_output: Walks the X-Y-Z-mechanism hypothesis form. Asks for the specific change (what is the current hero, what is the proposed hero), the metric expected to move, the expected lift band, and the mechanism. Provides a worked example. Notes that 'try a new hero' is not a hypothesis.
assertions: Walks the X-Y-Z-mechanism hypothesis form
Asks for the specific change and mechanism
Provides a worked example
Notes that vague 'try a new hero' is not a hypothesis
Asks about the expected lift band

evals.prompt: Our test ran for 4 weeks and the variant 'tied' with control. The product team wants to ship the variant anyway. Thoughts?
evals.expected_output: Pushback. Walks the consider-negative framework: a tie at adequate sample size means the variant did not produce the expected lift, which is information. Recommends not shipping unless there's a non-conversion reason (brand consistency, technical debt cleanup). Documents the result and schedules a 6-month retest if the team still wants to revisit.
assertions: Pushes back on shipping a tied variant by default
Walks the consider-negative framework
Notes a tie is information, not permission to ship
Recommends shipping only if non-conversion reason exists
Recommends documenting the result and scheduling a retest

evals.prompt: We want to test a new H1 AND a new CTA on the same PDP at the same time. Good idea?
evals.expected_output: Bad idea unless designed as a factorial. Walks the test isolation framework. Notes that two simultaneous tests on the same page contaminate each other's measurement. Recommends sequential testing (H1 first, ship the winner, then test CTA) or proper 2x2 factorial design with the right analysis.
assertions: Pushes back on simultaneous unrelated tests on same page
Walks the test isolation framework
Recommends sequential testing as the default
Mentions 2x2 factorial as the rare alternative for true simultaneity
Notes contamination of measurement

evals.prompt: We shipped a winner 18 months ago. The dashboard still shows we're up. Are we good?
evals.expected_output: Should retest. Walks the ship-the-winner-retest-in-6-months framework. Notes that customer behavior shifts and accumulated winners can silently turn into losers over time. Recommends retesting on a 6-month cadence, not 18-month. Asks whether the lift in production replicates the experimental lift.
assertions: Recommends retesting the 18-month-old winner
Walks the ship-and-retest framework
Names the 6-month retest cadence
Asks whether production lift replicates experimental lift
Notes that accumulated winners can silently turn into losers

evals.prompt: What's the minimum sample size for a subscription trial-to-paid conversion test? Current rate is 45%.
evals.expected_output: Walks the MDE-sample-size relationship at 45% baseline. At higher baseline conversion rates, less traffic is needed: a 5% relative lift (45% to 47.25%) needs ~3,000 trials per variant. Recommends the test as feasible if monthly trial volume permits. References subscription-growth for the trial-mechanics context.
assertions: Walks the MDE-sample-size relationship at 45% baseline
Computes required sample size for the lift band
Notes higher baselines need less traffic than rare-event baselines
References subscription-growth for trial-mechanics context
Recommends test as feasible if trial volume sufficient
